preFix.title: Building a simple workshop workbench
preFix.subtitle: Construct a robust timber workbench with a flat top, shelf, and leg vise position from standard construction timber
preFix.excerpt: How to build a basic workbench from PAR softwood and sheet materials. Covers the frame joints, fixing the top, bracing for rigidity, and the dimensions that make a bench comfortable to use for hand-tool work.
preFix.sourceNotes: Newey & Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Carpentry for Boys (1914). Collins Complete DIY Manual.

content.text: A good workbench is not complicated. The core requirements are: a flat top heavy enough not to move, legs that don't rack sideways, and a
that suits your build. This design uses full-length 100 × 50 mm PAR softwood for the frame and a glued-up sheet of 18 mm plywood or MDF for the top. It is a two-day build.
For a bench 1800 mm × 600 mm: four 100 × 50 mm softwood legs at your required height (typically 850 mm). Four rails, two long at 1700 mm and two short at 500 mm, in 100 × 50 mm. One
rail 1700 × 100 × 50 mm at the front. One sheet of 18 mm plywood 1800 × 600 mm for the top, glued and screwed to the frame. A lower shelf from a second sheet of 18 mm plywood.
Step 1, Cut the legs and rails

Cut all components to length. Check the legs are all the same length by measuring and marking all four at once, then sawing. Small variations in leg length are harder to fix than getting them right at the cutting stage, a bench that rocks is worse than no bench. Mark the top of each leg with the combination square and a bold pencil line so you know which end is which.

Fix two legs to a short rail using halving joints at the top (see the halving-joint technique tutorial) and the lower shelf rail, positioned 200 mm from the floor, fixed with screws and PVA glue. Clamp with the sash cramp while the glue sets. Check the frame for square by measuring corner to corner diagonally, equal diagonals mean the frame is square. Repeat for the second end frame.
Step 3, Connect the end frames with long rails

Lay both end frames on the floor. Fix the two long rails between them, one front, one back, at the same height as the short top rails, using screws and glue into the leg faces. Fit the front apron rail below the front top rail. Before glue sets, stand the bench upright and check for racking by pushing the top from side to side. Add a diagonal brace from an offcut under the lower shelf if the frame racks.
Step 4, Fit the top and shelf

Place the plywood top on the frame. Screw through the plywood from above into the rails on a 200 mm grid, countersink so the screw heads are below the surface. Fit the lower shelf plywood similarly. Sand the top surface with 80-grit paper wrapped around the sanding block to smooth any joint ridges, then seal with a coat of Danish oil, linseed oil, or exterior varnish to prevent the top absorbing glue and marking from tools.
Folding wall-mounted bench: a single-leaf bench on piano hinges that folds flat against the wall when not in use. Uses the same rail and top construction; no lower shelf.

Hardwood top: a glued-up hardwood-strip top (beech or maple, 50 to 75 mm thick) is durable but heavy and expensive. For hand-tool work, a plywood top with a solid-wood edge strip is a practical alternative.

items.fix: Fit a plywood or MDF back panel, or a diagonal timber brace, from one long rail to the opposite long rail on the lower shelf. Fix with screws and glue.
items.cause: No diagonal bracing in the frame; screws alone cannot resist racking loads

items.fix: Add additional screws on a tighter grid. For a permanently heavy-use bench, add a second layer of 18 mm plywood to the top.
items.cause: Too few screws fixing the top to the frame, or the frame rails too widely spaced
items.symptom: Top bounces or flexes under heavy planing

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
